selection_criteria.severity: How badly does this flaw break the system or block progress?
selection_criteria.frequency: How many files/modules are affected? How often is it encountered?
selection_criteria.testability: How severely does this flaw prevent isolated, repeatable testing?

top_3_flaws.title: God Class: MissionAdapter owns 8 responsibilities in 921 lines
top_3_flaws.explanation: MissionAdapter (adapter.py, 921 lines) violates the Single Responsibility Principle catastrophically. It bundles LLM planning (_plan_mission, ~150 lines), codebase exploration (_explore_codebase, ~60 lines), worker prompt assembly (_build_worker_prompt, ~50 lines), an LLM worker loop (_llm_worker, ~130 lines), three verification levels as static methods (L1/L2/L3, ~130 lines total), project memory updates (_update_memory_from_tool, ~40 lines), and permission setup — all in one class. The verifiers being static methods means no verification level can be tested in isolation without instantiating the entire adapter. Adding a new verification level (e.g., security scan) requires modifying MissionAdapter itself, violating the Open-Closed Principle.

impact.testability_blocked: Cannot unit-test verifiers, planner, or worker loop independently. Requires a real LLM and full adapter instantiation for any test.
impact.maintainability: Any change to planning, verification, or worker logic risks merge conflicts in the same 921-line file. New team members face a steep onboarding curve.
impact.cascading_effects: Root cause behind ARCH-F4 (concrete dependencies), ARCH-F5 (Orchestrator SRP), and ARCH-F6 (magic verifier keys). Decomposing this class would naturally force extraction of downstream abstractions.

top_3_flaws.fix_effort: High (3-5 days): Extract MissionPlanner, CodebaseExplorer, LlmWorker, StaticVerifier, TestVerifier, CodeReviewVerifier each behind a protocol, with MissionAdapter becoming a thin facade.

top_3_flaws.title: Global Mutable Singletons prevent parallel testing and hide coupling
top_3_flaws.explanation: Three module-level global variables serve as 'poor man's dependency injection' across four core files: _tracker in tracker.py (line 322), _smart_coordinator in smart_coordinator.py (line 55), and _auto_config in auto_config.py (line 35). get_tracker(), get_smart_coordinator(), and get_auto_config() are factory functions that return the module-level singleton. reset_tracker() exists precisely because the authors know the singleton breaks tests — it is a workaround for a design flaw, not a solution. Orchestrator.__init__ calls get_tracker() internally rather than accepting it via constructor injection. Parallel test runs share mutable state through these globals, producing non-deterministic failures.

impact.testability_blocked: Parallel tests share mutable tracker/coordinator/config state. reset_tracker() must be called between test cases. CI pipelines with pytest-xdist will experience flaky, non-reproducible failures.
impact.maintainability: Hidden coupling: any module can call get_tracker() without the dependency being visible in the constructor signature. Refactoring tracker internals requires auditing all call sites across the codebase.
impact.cascading_effects: Encourages the same anti-pattern elsewhere. New developers see reset_tracker() as a legitimate pattern rather than a code smell.

src/pilotcode/orchestration/orchestrator.anti_pattern: self.tracker = get_tracker(db_path=self.config.db_path) — internal singleton fetch instead of constructor injection

top_3_flaws.fix_effort: Medium (1-2 days): Constructor injection throughout. Remove get_tracker(), get_smart_coordinator(), and the module-level _auto_config. Let the application entry point create and wire instances.

top_3_flaws.title: Concrete infrastructure dependencies make MissionAdapter untestable without a real LLM
top_3_flaws.explanation: MissionAdapter imports concrete infrastructure directly at the module level rather than accepting dependencies via constructor injection: get_model_client (adapter.py:15), QueryEngine + QueryEngineConfig (line 16), get_all_tools (line 17), and get_permission_manager (line 22). These are called inside _llm_worker, _plan_mission, and _code_review_verifier — the three most critical methods. Writing a unit test for _plan_mission requires either a real LLM (slow, costly, non-deterministic) or unittest.mock.patch across five module paths (fragile, breaks on any import refactor). This is a direct Dependency Inversion Principle violation: high-level policy (mission planning) depends on low-level infrastructure (concrete LLM client, concrete tool registry), not on abstractions.

impact.testability_blocked: Cannot unit-test _plan_mission, _llm_worker, or _code_review_verifier without either a real LLM (costly, slow, non-deterministic) or fragile mock.patch chains across 5+ module paths. Test suites are slow and flaky.
impact.maintainability: Swapping the LLM provider, tool registry, or permission system requires editing MissionAdapter internals rather than passing a different implementation at construction time.
impact.cascading_effects: Downstream of ARCH-F1 (God Class). Because MissionAdapter is already a god class, the concrete imports are buried inside methods rather than being constructor parameters. Fixing F1 naturally forces fixing F4.

top_3_flaws.fix_effort: Medium (1-2 days): Accept model_client, tool_registry, and permission_manager as constructor arguments typed to protocols/ABCs. The application entry point wires concrete implementations.

justification_summary: ARCH-F1 (God Class) is ranked #1 because it is the root cause behind F4, F5, and F6 per the architectural compliance report. Its 921-line, 8-responsibility class blocks all isolated testing of verification, planning, and worker logic. ARCH-F2 (Global Singletons) is ranked #2 because it affects 4 files with 3 mutable globals, directly causes flaky parallel tests, and the reset_tracker() workaround is an explicit admission of the design flaw. ARCH-F4 (Concrete Dependencies) is ranked #3 over ARCH-F3 (duplicate worker selection) because F4 is a direct testability blocker — you simply cannot write a fast, deterministic unit test for the planner or worker without either a real LLM or fragile mocking — whereas F3's duplicated logic can at least be tested independently in each location. F4 is also downstream of F1, so fixing F1 naturally addresses F4.
not_selected_but_notable: ARCH-F3 (Duplicate worker-type selection in orchestrator.py:323 and context_strategy.py:347) — High severity DRY violation that could cause divergent behavior, but the two implementations are independently testable and the fix is low-effort (2-4 hours).
ARCH-F5 (Orchestrator mixes scheduling, verification, and retry in 720 lines) — Medium severity SRP violation; becomes straightforward to fix once F1 and F2 are addressed.
ARCH-F6 (Magic integer verifier keys violate OCP) — Medium severity; adding a security scan verifier requires touching 4 files. Fix is low-effort (enum + pipeline pattern).
SMELL-003 (Duplicate MockAgent in examples) — High severity in example code but confined to demo files, not core orchestration logic.
recommended_fix_order: Step 1: Extract MissionAdapter into focused classes (fix ARCH-F1), introducing constructor-injected dependencies (fix ARCH-F4 simultaneously).
Step 2: Replace global singletons with constructor injection (fix ARCH-F2).
Step 3: After F1/F2/F4 are resolved, ARCH-F3, F5, and F6 become low-effort cleanup tasks.
